terrorist-attacks-fatalities-and-injurie:
Terrorist attacks, deaths and injuries

Confirmed deaths and injuries include all victims and attackers who died or were hurt because of a terrorist attack.

This line chart tracks global totals from 1970 to 2021 on a vertical axis scaled from 0 to 50,000. Three series are plotted: deaths in brown, non-fatal injuries in red, and attacks in blue. Through the 1970s all three lines stay near the baseline, mostly below 5,000. During the 1980s the brown and red lines climb to oscillate between roughly 5,000 and 10,000, while the blue line for attacks remains lower at around 2,000 to 5,000. The 1990s hold a similar range, with a small dip just before 2000. A sharp isolated spike in the red non-fatal injuries line reaches close to 28,000 near 2001, while the other two series stay near 5,000 that year. From around 2004 onward all three series rise together, with the steepest climb between 2011 and 2014. The peak of the chart occurs around 2014, where deaths reach about 45,000 and non-fatal injuries reach roughly 44,000, tracking almost on top of each other. The blue attacks line peaks lower, near 17,000, in the same interval. After 2014 all three series fall: by 2021 deaths sit near 23,000, non-fatal injuries near 21,000, and attacks near 8,000. Across the full span, the attacks line remains consistently below the two casualty lines once the three diverge in the mid-1980s.
number-of-new-state-based-conflicts:
Number of new state-based conflicts

Included are interstate, intrastate, and extrasystemic conflicts that started that year.

This stacked bar chart plots annual counts from 1946 to 2024, with the vertical axis running from 0 to 18. The legend lists four stacked categories: internationalized intrastate in orange, non-internationalized intrastate in green, extrasystemic in dark red, and interstate in blue-grey. The tallest bar sits at 1991, reaching 18 and composed mostly of a large green segment with an orange cap and a small blue-grey base. A second tall bar of 16 stands at 2015, dominated by a long orange segment on top of a shorter green base. Other prominent bars include 1946 at 11, with mixed blue-grey, dark red and green segments, and 1975 at 10, primarily green. Bars around 1956 and 1966 reach 7, and the late 1960s and early 1970s contain several bars in the 5 to 7 range with green and dark red components. The late 1950s and 1960s show recurring dark red extrasystemic segments, while blue-grey interstate segments appear intermittently across the 1940s, 1960s, and 1980s. Through the 2000s many bars fall to 3 or below, with 2007 sitting as a short blue-grey bar. From 2011 onward orange internationalized intrastate segments grow more prominent, and the final bar at 2024 stands around 3 with a blue-grey base and a green top. A note under the chart states that some conflicts affect several regions and do not necessarily start at the same time across them, so the sum across all regions can be higher than the global number.
production-printed-books-half-century:
Production of printed books per half century

A book is defined as a published title with more than 49 pages.

This line chart plots production from 1475 to 1775 on a vertical axis marked from 0 to 160 million, with observations positioned at the midpoint of each 50-year period per the note. Labeled series include France, Great Britain, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Sweden, Ireland, Spain, Other countries, Russia, Poland, Belgium, and Switzerland. All lines start close to the baseline around 1475. Italy rises first and leads through the sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries, climbing to about 85 million at the 1675 midpoint before dipping to roughly 38 million around 1725 and recovering to about 75 million by 1775. Great Britain grows steadily, plateaus near 90 million between 1675 and 1725, then jumps to about 140 million by 1775. France stays moderate through 1675, then accelerates to finish at roughly 160 million, the highest point on the chart. Germany tracks a similar shape and ends near 120 million. The Netherlands rises gradually and reaches about 55 million at the final point. A dense cluster of lines for Sweden, Ireland, Spain, Other countries, Russia, Poland, Belgium, and Switzerland sits in the lower portion, mostly under 20 million until the final period, when the Other countries line lifts toward roughly 22 million and Sweden, Ireland, and Spain curl upward to around 15 to 20 million. Russia, Poland, Belgium, and Switzerland remain nearest the axis at the end of the span.
women-required-to-obey-husband:
Married women are required by law to obey their husbands

Any provision stating that married women must obey their husband or if disobeying their husband has legal ramifications for them, such as loss of their right to maintenance.

This choropleth world map shows the status for 2023. The legend lists three categories: No data shown with diagonal hatching, Yes shown in orange, and No shown in purple. A cluster of orange countries spans North Africa and the Arabian Peninsula, including Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya, Egypt, Sudan, Saudi Arabia, Yemen, Oman, and the United Arab Emirates, along with neighbouring Mauritania, Mali, and Niger in the Sahel. Additional orange countries appear in the Middle East and western Asia, and Malaysia and Indonesia are shaded orange in Southeast Asia. The remainder of the map is coloured purple, covering all of the Americas, Europe, the rest of Sub-Saharan Africa, Russia, India, China, Japan, Australia, and New Zealand. Greenland is rendered with visible diagonal hatching, denoting no data. Most country borders elsewhere are drawn cleanly without hatching. The orange Yes category forms a contiguous east-west band through northern Africa and the Middle East with a secondary pocket in maritime Southeast Asia, while the purple No category covers the rest of the globe.
incidence-rate-of-cardiovascular-disease:
Incidence rate of cardiovascular diseases

Estimated share of the population who newly develop cardiovascular diseases, in a given year.

This choropleth world map shows values for 2023 on a legend ranging from 0.5% to 0.9%, coloured from pale yellow at the low end through orange to dark red at the high end. The note states that the metric is age-standardized. The darkest red shading, near 0.9%, covers Egypt, along with a small cluster of countries in the Caucasus region. Ukraine and neighbouring parts of eastern Europe sit in dark orange bands close to 0.8%. Russia is shaded mid-orange across its span. Much of central Asia, the Middle East, and North Africa falls in the mid-orange range corresponding to values between about 0.6% and 0.8%. Sub-Saharan Africa is a patchwork of light to mid oranges. North America is shaded in light to mid orange tones. Western Europe shows a mix of pale yellow and light orange. South America is predominantly pale yellow, with Argentina and Chile at the lightest end near 0.5%. Australia, New Zealand, and much of Southeast Asia also sit in pale yellow shades. China is shaded in a light to mid orange. Greenland and a few countries in central Africa are drawn with diagonal hatching marking no data. The overall pattern places the highest shares across a band running from eastern Europe through the Caucasus and into North Africa, while the southern hemisphere and parts of western Europe hold the lowest shares.
income-inequality-in-market-and-disposab:
Income inequality in market and disposable income across the world

Unit 5 'Property and power: Mutual gains and conflict' Section 5.12 'Measuring economic inequality' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at: https://tinyco.re/05121660 [Figure 5.16]

This scatter plot places Market Gini on the horizontal axis from 0 to about 0.7 and Disposable Gini on the vertical axis from 0 to about 0.6, with a dotted diagonal reference line running from the origin. Points are colour-coded by region in a legend naming North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. South Africa sits alone at the far upper right, with a Market Gini near 0.71 and a Disposable Gini near 0.62, above the diagonal. China (People's Republic of) and Brazil cluster to the right, each with Market Gini near 0.58 to 0.60 and Disposable Gini near 0.47 to 0.52. India and Chile sit slightly lower, with Market Gini around 0.54 to 0.55 and Disposable Gini near 0.46 to 0.50. Mexico is labelled further left, with Market Gini around 0.43 and Disposable Gini about 0.42. A dense cluster of European and Oceania points fills the centre, with Market Gini mostly between 0.42 and 0.55 and Disposable Gini between 0.25 and 0.40; labelled members include Bulgaria, Lithuania, Austria, Australia, Norway, and Iceland. Korea sits left of centre near 0.34 on the Disposable axis. Most points lie below the diagonal, indicating Disposable Gini values lower than Market Gini for those entries. The note states that Disposable income Gini is displayed on the 'MAP' tab.
suicide-rate-in-1980-vs-2023:
Suicide rate in 1980 vs. 2023

Estimated aumber of suicides per 100,000 people in a given population and year. Suicide deaths are underreported in
many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide
patterns, and includes adjustments that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

This scatter plot places the 2023 rate on the horizontal axis from 0 to roughly 27 and the 1980 rate on the vertical axis from 0 to 35, with age-standardization noted below. Circle size encodes population, with an inset key showing 800M and 200M reference circles, and colours mark continent in the legend: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. Hungary sits highest on the vertical axis near 36 with a 2023 rate around 12. Slovenia lies close behind near 32 on the 1980 axis. Russia plots near 33 in 1980 and about 19 in 2023, with Lithuania nearby around 31 and 19. Sri Lanka appears near 29 and 14, and Denmark near 27 and 8. China is drawn as a large teal circle at roughly 5 for 2023 and 23 for 1980. Kazakhstan, Latvia, and Austria cluster in the 23 to 27 range for 1980 with 2023 values from 9 to 14. Eritrea, Lesotho, and Guyana sit on the right side with 2023 values between 20 and 27 and 1980 values between 19 and 23. India plots as a large teal circle near 13 and 10. France, Germany, Norway, and the United Kingdom group in the middle with 1980 values between 10 and 20 and 2023 values between 7 and 13. Qatar, Indonesia, Bangladesh, Puerto Rico, Mexico, Brazil, Argentina, and Paraguay sit in the lower left with both values below 10.
